items.title: Did Investors Get Too Far Ahead of the Artificial Intelligence (AI) Revolution? The Market Is Starting to Say Yes.
items.description: The article argues that AI stocks may be experiencing a bubble similar to the dot-com crash, with investors getting ahead of the technology's actual value. While AI is a powerful and transformative technology, historical patterns show that powerful technologies often lead to investment excess. Recent signs include Nvidia and AI-focused stocks declining significantly, with some AI darlings like SoundHound down 75% from 2025 highs. Combined with recession concerns driven by geopolitical conflicts and rising energy prices, the author suggests investors should consider taking profits given the increased risk.
items.author: Reuben Gregg Brewer

insights.reasoning: Stock down 17% from 2025 highs and identified as the poster child for AI revolution that may be overvalued; represents broader concerns about AI bubble and correction territory
Down roughly 17% from highs, mirroring Nvidia's decline; represents collection of high-profile AI-driven stocks that may be overvalued relative to fundamentals
Down approximately 75% from 2025 highs; identified as an early AI market darling now deeply out of favor, representing the highest-risk stocks that break first when bubbles burst
Down approximately 75% from 2025 highs; identified as an early AI market darling now deeply out of favor, representing the highest-risk stocks that break first when bubbles burst

items.title: 3 Artificial Intelligence (AI) Stocks to Buy at a Discount
items.description: The article recommends three AI-related stocks trading at discounts: Micron Technology, which has surged 300% in a year but trades at only 6.8x forward earnings due to market skepticism about sustained memory chip demand; Nvidia, growing at ~77% with a valuation of 21.1x forward earnings compared to historical 30-40x multiples for similar growth rates; and Microsoft, which shows strong fundamentals with 17% revenue growth and 60% EPS growth, trading at historically low valuation levels.
items.author: Keithen Drury

insights.reasoning: Stock is trading at a significant discount (6.8x forward earnings) despite 300% year-over-year gains and explosive revenue growth (from $8B to $23.9B quarterly). Supply constraints and high demand for memory chips present a strong buying opportunity if the shortage persists.

Exceptional growth of 73-77% year-over-year with valuation of 21.1x forward earnings, only slightly above S&P 500 average despite growth rates historically commanding 30-40x multiples. Strong AI hardware demand expected through 2030 makes it a compelling value.

Solid fundamentals with 17% revenue growth and 60% EPS growth, trading at historically low valuation levels since the start of the decade. Strong position in AI arms race with OpenAI investment provides long-term growth potential.

items.title: This AI Giant Aims to Reach $9 Trillion in Market Cap. Is the Stock a Buy?
items.description: Meta Platforms has offered executives stock options tied to reaching a $9 trillion market valuation by 2031, signaling ambitious AI growth plans. While the $9 trillion goal appears mathematically unlikely based on current revenue projections and valuation multiples, the author recommends buying Meta stock due to its cheap valuation at 19x forward earnings, strong profitability from its social media advertising business, and potential upside from AI investments.
items.author: Adria Cimino

insights.reasoning: Despite skepticism about the $9 trillion goal being unrealistic, the author recommends buying the stock, citing attractive valuation at 19x forward earnings, strong profitability from advertising, and significant upside potential from AI initiatives. The company's commitment to AI development and talent retention is viewed favorably.
Mentioned as a peer company that previously held the position of world's largest company by market value. No specific analysis or recommendation provided.
Mentioned as a peer company that previously held the position of world's largest company by market value. No specific analysis or recommendation provided.
Mentioned as the current world's largest company by market value and first to reach $4 trillion market cap. Serves as context for Meta's ambitions but receives no specific analysis.

items.title: 10 AI Stocks I'm Buying Right Now
items.description: The article presents 10 AI stocks recommended for investment, divided into three categories: AI chip manufacturers (Nvidia, Broadcom, Taiwan Semiconductor), AI hyperscalers (Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta), and emerging AI plays (IonQ, Nebius, SoundHound AI). The author highlights strong growth prospects across the sector, noting that several large-cap stocks are down from all-time highs, presenting buying opportunities.
items.author: Keithen Drury

insights.reasoning: Strong revenue growth (73% YoY in Q4, 77% expected in Q1), dominant position in GPU market for AI training/inference, though stock recently lackluster presenting buying opportunity

items.title: Nvidia May be Back in Business in China. Here's What That Means for Revenue.
items.description: Nvidia has received U.S. approval to resume chip sales to China after a year-long absence due to export controls. The company plans to ship H200 chips within weeks and aims to gain approval for more powerful Blackwell chips by next year. China represented 13% of Nvidia's revenue in fiscal 2025 (approximately $28 billion annually), with potential to grow significantly as the company restarts production and exports.
items.author: Adria Cimino

insights.reasoning: Nvidia received regulatory approval to resume lucrative China sales after a year-long ban, with plans to ship H200 chips within weeks. China represents a significant revenue opportunity (~$28 billion annually based on historical 13% share), and the company's CEO expressed optimism about market potential reaching hundreds of billions by decade's end. However, sentiment is tempered by production ramp-up challenges and increased local competition during Nvidia's absence.

items.title: Here's Why The Vanguard S&P 500 Growth ETF Could Be Your Ticket to Beating the Market Over the Long Term
items.description: The Vanguard S&P 500 Growth ETF (VOOG) has outperformed the S&P 500 for 16 years by focusing on 139 high-performing growth stocks, particularly in technology. With a 47% allocation to information technology and heavy weightings in companies like Nvidia and Microsoft, the ETF delivered 16.3% annual returns versus 14% for the S&P 500. Though it's down 7.1% in 2026 amid market volatility, the article suggests it remains a good long-term buy for investors with a 5+ year horizon.
items.author: Anthony Di Pizio

insights.reasoning: The ETF has consistently outperformed the S&P 500 for 16 years with a 16.3% compound annual return versus 14% for the broader index. Despite current 2026 weakness, the article recommends it as a strong long-term buy for investors with 5+ year horizons.
Highlighted as a top holding in VOOG with a median return of 1,400% over the last decade, representing a major driver of the ETF's outperformance.
Listed as a top holding with exceptional 1,400% median returns over the last decade, contributing significantly to VOOG's outperformance.

items.title: There Are 10 Trillion-Dollar Stocks On the Market. This Is the Only One That's Up This Year
items.description: While tech stocks have tumbled 12% this year amid AI disruption concerns and geopolitical tensions from the Iran war, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSMC) stands out as the only trillion-dollar stock with gains, up 7.5% year-to-date. TSMC's strong competitive moat, with over 90% of advanced third-party chip production, positions it well to benefit from the $700 billion in hyperscaler capital expenditures planned for AI infrastructure.
items.author: Jeremy Bowman

insights.reasoning: TSMC is the only trillion-dollar stock up this year (+7.5%), demonstrating resilience amid tech sector decline. Strong competitive moat with 90% of advanced third-party chip production, 26% revenue growth, 54% operating margin, and reasonable P/E of 31.5 support continued growth prospects in AI infrastructure spending.

Mentioned as facing AI disruption concerns and overspending on capital expenditures. Part of the broader tech sector decline, though still a major customer of TSMC with expected $1 trillion revenue over two years.
Listed among fabless chip companies exposed to AI sector risks. Part of the tech sector decline amid concerns about AI bubble and software disruption.
Mentioned as a fabless chip company with AI exposure facing sector headwinds and concerns about potential demand pullback.
Explicitly noted as having fallen further behind TSMC in semiconductor production capabilities, indicating competitive disadvantage in the advanced chip market.

items.title: Every Magnificent Seven Stock Is Down This Year. This One Is a Screaming Buy
items.description: All Magnificent Seven stocks have underperformed the S&P 500 in 2026 amid concerns about AI spending and disruption. However, valuations have become attractive, with most trading at par with the broader market. Nvidia stands out as the best opportunity, trading at a forward P/E of less than 21 while maintaining the fastest growth in the group and strong revenue acceleration.
items.author: Jeremy Bowman

insights.reasoning: Despite current underperformance, Nvidia is highlighted as a 'screaming buy' with attractive forward P/E of less than 21, fastest growth in the Mag Seven, accelerating revenue growth, and CEO guidance of $1 trillion revenue over two years, suggesting strong long-term potential.
Part of the Magnificent Seven that has underperformed the S&P 500 this year with valuations now at par with the market, but continues to deliver double-digit revenue growth.
One of the top four hyperscalers facing investor skepticism over massive AI capital expenditures (~$700 billion combined), but maintains sector leadership and double-digit revenue growth.

items.title: Wall Street Insider Who Predicted 2008 Crash Issues Urgent Warning: "The AI Bubble Will Pop by April 29th"
items.description: Economist Jim Rickards, who predicted the 2008 financial crisis and COVID market crash, warns that an AI bubble is heading for catastrophic collapse by April 29th, 2026. He claims a major AI company with $1.4 trillion in financial entanglements could trigger an 80% market crash similar to 2008. Elite investors including Peter Thiel, SoftBank, Ray Dalio, and Warren Buffett are reportedly reducing their tech holdings, signaling preparation for an AI sector meltdown.
items.author: Jim Rickards

insights.reasoning: Major investors including Peter Thiel, SoftBank, and Ray Dalio are significantly reducing or exiting their Nvidia positions, cited as evidence of smart money preparing for an AI sector collapse.
Ray Dalio's hedge fund cut its Google position by 52%, and the presentation suggests Google's technology will guarantee failure of a major AI competitor, indicating negative outlook.

Ray Dalio's hedge fund reduced its Meta position by 48%, signaling reduced confidence in the company amid broader AI sector concerns.
